file_name: Gaytán-Campos et al. - 2022 - Automatic classification of images with skin cancer using artificial intelligence.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper implemented artificial intelligence and machine learning techniques for the automatic classification of skin cancer images. Specifically, it used traditional machine learning algorithms including Decision Trees, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Naive Bayes, k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN), and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN). Additionally, it employed Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for image feature extraction. The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset to classify three types of skin cancer: melanocytic nevus, melanoma, and benign keratosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation of a block programming model for skin cancer image classification. It details specific methodologies including the use of the HAM10000 dataset, application of image filters (grayscale, Canny, etc.), and evaluation of multiple machine learning algorithms (SVM, k-NN, DT, NB, ANN) and CNN feature extractors. The study reports new quantitative results, including precision and accuracy metrics in Tables 1-5, and compares performance across different classification scenarios, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Automatic Classification of Images with Skin Cancer Using Artificial Intelligence
brief_summary: This study implemented a block programming model for the automatic classification of skin cancer images using traditional machine learning algorithms and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for feature extraction. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset, focusing on three specific classes: melanocytic nevus, melanoma, and benign keratosis. The methodology involved applying various image filters, such as grayscale and Canny operators, to the original images before classification. Performance was evaluated using precision metrics across two scenarios: an 80-20 training-test split and 10-fold cross-validation. The results indicated that unfiltered original images generally yielded the highest precision, with the Artificial Neural Network and Support Vector Machine achieving the best overall performance. The authors concluded that the proposed classifier serves as a viable support tool for dermatological diagnosis, although filter application did not significantly improve classification values.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes selected from the HAM10000 dataset for the classification experiment. 200 images were used. Renamed to 'B' in the A-B-C classification scenario and part of the 'BC' group in the A-BC scenario.

One of the three classes selected from the HAM10000 dataset for the classification experiment. 200 images were used. Renamed to 'A' in the A-B-C classification scenario and the 'A' group in the A-BC scenario.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin cancer lesions (melanocytic nevus, melanoma, and benign keratosis) mapped to classes A, B, and C.
all_task_types.details: The paper implements supervised machine learning algorithms (Decision Trees, SVM, Naive Bayes, k-NN, ANN) and CNN feature extraction for the automatic classification of skin cancer images from the HAM10000 dataset. The task involves assigning images to discrete classes (A-B-C, A-BC, B-AC, C-AB configurations) to distinguish between benign and malignant lesions. Performance was evaluated using precision and accuracy metrics, not continuous regression targets.

datasets_used.name: Skin Cancer MNIST: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-resource dermoscopy images of pigmented lesions. The study selected 3 specific classes from this dataset: melanocytic nevi (Nv2), melanoma (Mel2), and benign lesions similar to keratosis (Bk2). The selected subset consisted of 600 images total, with 200 images per class.

use.training: Used for training machine learning classifiers (SVM, k-NN, DT, NB, ANN) and CNN feature extraction. Split into 80% training and 20% testing in one scenario, and used in 10-fold cross-validation in another.
use.validation: Used for validation via 10-fold cross-validation scenario.
use.testing: Used for testing in the 80-20 split scenario and 10-fold cross-validation scenario.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for data training and evaluation to assess model performance stability.
Used to divide the dataset into training (80%) and testing (20%) sets for model evaluation.
Used to evaluate and compare the performance of different classification algorithms and image processing filters.
Used to evaluate model performance, specifically noting the best accuracy of 76.4% for SVM in the A-B-C scenario.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using precision and accuracy metrics across different classification scenarios (80-20 split and 10-fold cross-validation) and image filters, but no statistical significance testing was applied to compare these results.

architecture_used.main_model: Traditional machine learning classifiers (Support Vector Machine, k-Nearest Neighbors, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes, Artificial Neural Network) combined with Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) feature extractors.
architecture_used.pretraining: CNN embedders were pre-trained on ImageNet (SqueezeNet, InceptionV3, VGG16, VGG19) or specific datasets (Painters on 79,433 painting images, DeepLoc on 21,882 cell images).
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported; images were processed using Orange Data Mining application.

feature_extractors: Painters (embedder trained on 79,433 images of paintings)
DeepLoc (CNN trained on 21,882 images of individual cells)
architecture_details.embedder_configuration: Activations from the penultimate layer of the CNNs were used as embeddings.

Naive Bayes (NB).preprocessing: Removal of empty columns, discretization of numerical values into 4 containers with same frequency

training_details.feature_extractor_selection: The 'Painters' embedder was selected for all classifiers as it showed the best performance in initial evaluations.

architecture_used.classification_or_output_head: Discrete classes (A, B, C) corresponding to Melanocytic nevus, Melanoma, and Benign keratosis.

preprocessing_techniques: Grayscale conversion (using weighted average: 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B)
Median filter (statistical nonlinear spatial filter to remove unwanted artifacts)
Binarization (transforming grayscale images to black and white)
Canny operator (edge detection algorithm for recovering contours)
Image Embedding using Orange Data Mining application (feature extraction/embedding)
conclusions: The proposed block programming model using artificial intelligence and machine learning is a viable support tool for dermatological diagnosis of skin cancer.
The application of image filters (such as grayscale, Canny, median, and binarization) did not significantly improve classification performance compared to using unfiltered original images.
Unfiltered original images generally yielded the highest precision values across the tested classification scenarios.
The Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers achieved the best overall performance among the tested traditional machine learning algorithms.
The 'Painters' Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) embedder was identified as the most effective feature extractor for the tested classification algorithms.

The study demonstrated that combining traditional machine learning classifiers with CNN-based feature extraction can effectively classify skin cancer images (melanocytic nevus, melanoma, and benign keratosis) from the HAM10000 dataset.
The methodology allows for technical assistance to dermatologists by reducing the time required for diagnosis without requiring high programming knowledge from the end-user.
important_remarks: The study used a highly restricted subset of the HAM10000 dataset, selecting only 600 images (200 per class) for three specific lesion types (melanocytic nevus, melanoma, benign keratosis), ignoring four other classes present in the source dataset.
The paper did not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to validate the significance of performance differences between algorithms or preprocessing methods.

Image preprocessing filters (grayscale, Canny, median, binarization) did not significantly improve classification performance compared to using unfiltered original images, suggesting that feature extraction via CNNs may render these specific filters redundant or potentially detrimental.

The study relied on traditional machine learning classifiers (SVM, k-NN, DT, NB, ANN) combined with CNN feature extractors rather than end-to-end deep learning models, which may limit the ability to capture complex hierarchical features compared to state-of-the-art deep learning approaches.

The 'Painters' CNN embedder (trained on painting images) was identified as the most effective feature extractor, which is an unusual finding compared to standard medical or natural image pre-training datasets like ImageNet.
The proposed method is positioned as a 'support tool' for dermatologists rather than a diagnostic replacement, acknowledging that accurate diagnosis still requires specialist expertise.

The study acknowledges that the current implementation requires programming knowledge, limiting its direct usability for non-technical dermatologists, although it aims to provide a block programming model to mitigate this.

Performance varied significantly across different classification scenarios (A-B-C, A-BC, B-AC, C-AB), with the B-AC category showing the highest performance and the C-AB category showing a drop in performance, indicating potential class imbalance or difficulty in distinguishing specific lesion pairs.
